name: Stem Cell Replacement and Transplantation Coverage Assessment for June 13, 2026

assessment_window.basis: Backfill assessment of the published June 1 stem-cell replacement and transplantation baseline plus the June 6 activity-only surveillance bundle published on June 7; no new external source search was run for this assessment.

summary: Coverage is adequate for the current low-confidence human_functional_benefit public claim because the local map contains a randomized older-adult frailty laromestrocel functional signal, small human Parkinson and retinal graft-survival or exploratory functional signals, a randomized multiple-sclerosis HSCT disease-outcome boundary, and a refreshed exPDite-2 phase 3 registry momentum signal. Coverage is not strong because the most directly aging-relevant MSC result may be paracrine or tissue-support biology rather than durable stem-cell replacement; the clearest engrafting replacement programs remain invasive, small, open-label, and disease-specific; the HSCT evidence is high-intensity immune ablation and reconstitution for multiple sclerosis rather than broad stem-cell-exhaustion reversal; animal lifespan or broad healthy-aging coverage is not mapped; active registries have no posted replacement-efficacy results; and recent-review or formal seminal-anchor triangulation has not been recorded. The June 6 surveillance pass found only an activity-date registry refresh, so ordinary surveillance can continue.

evidence_categories.rationale: The baseline includes the main human intervention anchors for the public stage: a randomized, blinded, placebo-controlled phase 2b laromestrocel trial in older adults with aging frailty; open-label bemdaneprocel and A9-DPC Parkinson disease graft trials with exploratory motor or PET signals; open-label hESC-derived retinal pigment epithelium transplantation with possible visual benefit; and a randomized nonmyeloablative HSCT multiple-sclerosis trial with disease-progression benefit.
evidence_categories.gap_note: Adequacy is limited to a low-confidence functional-benefit claim; independent replication, durable older-adult function, and broader aging-relevant outcomes remain missing.

evidence_categories.rationale: The assessed artifacts are intervention and registry centered. They do not systematically map observational cohorts, natural-history comparators, transplant registry follow-up, graft-function trajectories, frailty natural experiments, or real-world safety and durability data.
evidence_categories.gap_note: This is not blocking for the current intervention-driven public stage, but future work could add useful context on durability, selection effects, procedure risk, and real-world implementation.

evidence_categories.rationale: The public baseline is driven by human functional and disease-specific intervention evidence. The assessed artifacts do not map animal lifespan, healthspan, stem-cell-exhaustion rescue, or broad tissue-replacement models.
evidence_categories.gap_note: A stronger LEV-relevant interpretation would need animal or human evidence that replacement strategies restore aged tissue function durably across contexts, not only disease-specific graft or transplant effects.

evidence_categories.rationale: Mechanistic coverage separates allogeneic MSC frailty therapy, hES or hESC-derived dopaminergic progenitor grafting, hESC-derived retinal pigment epithelium transplantation, phase 3 bemdaneprocel registry momentum, and immune-ablation plus hematopoietic reconstitution in multiple sclerosis. The outlook and reviews explicitly preserve the difference between paracrine MSC effects, literal engrafting replacement, and high-intensity transplantation.
evidence_categories.gap_note: Mechanistic adequacy does not prove durable replacement of aged stem-cell reserves, and it does not collapse MSC support, neural grafting, retinal grafting, and HSCT into one general rejuvenation mechanism.

evidence_categories.rationale: The public outlook and reviews preserve important limits: laromestrocel needs independent replication and may not be true replacement; Parkinson and retinal graft data are small, open-label, procedure-intensive, and disease-specific; exPDite-2 is recruiting with no posted results; and HSCT is an intensive disease-specific transplant boundary rather than a broad aging intervention.
evidence_categories.gap_note: A broader failed-program, discontinuation, or long-term graft-complication map has not been reconciled.

evidence_categories.rationale: The baseline captures early safety signals and explicit procedure boundaries for MSC infusion, dopaminergic progenitor grafting, retinal grafting, and HSCT. It does not deeply map long-term graft behavior, tumor risk, ectopic differentiation, dyskinesia, rejection, immunosuppression, conditioning toxicity, infection, clonal expansion, repeat dosing, durability, or frail older-adult implementation burden.
evidence_categories.gap_note: Do not strengthen confidence or LEV relevance without longer-term safety and durability evidence tied to clinically meaningful benefit.

evidence_categories.rationale: The June 6 surveillance pass directly checked baseline laromestrocel, bemdaneprocel phase 1, A9-DPC, retinal RPE, HSCT/MS, and exPDite-2 registries, plus broader registry searches. The only material same-window delta was the exPDite-2 NCT06944522 last-update-posted date; the trial remained recruiting with estimated enrollment of 102 and no posted results.
evidence_categories.gap_note: Continue watching exPDite-2, laromestrocel frailty replication, Parkinson graft programs, retinal replacement trials, and HSCT or immune-reconstitution comparators for posted results, status changes, safety, and durability.

evidence_categories.rationale: The backfill reviewed local bootstrap and surveillance artifacts. The June 6 surveillance pass found review-level context, including a broad regenerative-medicine review and a kidney-transplant preservation systematic review, but no formal review, meta-analysis, guideline, or comparative-effectiveness triangulation has been reconciled against the six-source baseline.
evidence_categories.gap_note: A future source-completeness pass should compare recent cell-replacement, MSC frailty, Parkinson graft, retinal graft, HSCT, and stem-cell-exhaustion reviews against the current source map.

evidence_categories.rationale: The baseline includes plausible public anchors for laromestrocel aging frailty, bemdaneprocel Parkinson grafting, A9-DPC Parkinson grafting, exPDite-2 phase 3 activity, hESC-derived retinal pigment epithelium transplantation, and nonmyeloablative HSCT in multiple sclerosis. These are sufficient for the current low-confidence public read.
evidence_categories.gap_note: Formal must-include, review-cited, guideline-cited, registry-network, or citation-network auditing could still change source-completeness confidence.

evidence_categories.rationale: The outlook, bootstrap notes, surveillance session, and reviews distinguish stem-cell replacement and transplantation from stem-cell rejuvenation, paracrine MSC tissue support, EV or secretome therapy, generic regenerative-medicine claims, disease-specific Parkinson or retinal replacement, and high-intensity HSCT immune reconstitution. The activity-only surveillance bundle also keeps exPDite-2 as momentum rather than efficacy evidence.
evidence_categories.gap_note: Boundary maintenance remains important because future MSC, EV, organoid, retinal, neural, immune, transplantation, and rejuvenation records may belong partly or primarily to neighboring tracks.

search_log_summary: This backfill reviewed local project artifacts: the published Stem Cell Replacement and Transplantation outlook, June 1 bootstrap session, published baseline candidate bundle, source-fidelity and interpretation reviews, baseline publication event, June 6 activity-only surveillance session, published surveillance candidate bundle, surveillance evidence reviews, and June 7 surveillance publication event. The surveillance log included PubMed, ClinicalTrials.gov, bioRxiv/medRxiv, web, sponsor, and correction/retraction checks through June 6. No new PubMed, registry, preprint, citation-network, or web source search was run for this assessment.
source_selection_notes: PMID 41747733 is the most directly aging-relevant human functional anchor because it reports laromestrocel-associated 6-minute walk improvement in older adults with aging frailty.
PMID 40240592 and PMID 41086804 anchor current Parkinson disease dopaminergic progenitor graft-survival and exploratory motor-signal evidence.

NCT06944522 is a momentum and activity source only: exPDite-2 remains a recruiting randomized, triple-masked, sham-surgery-controlled phase 3 trial with no posted results as of the June 5, 2026 ClinicalTrials.gov update.
PMID 25458728 anchors early hESC-derived retinal pigment epithelium transplantation safety, graft-survival, and possible visual-function support in retinal disease.
PMID 30644983 anchors the high-intensity transplantation boundary with randomized multiple-sclerosis progression benefit after nonmyeloablative HSCT.
The June 6 surveillance pass found no material post-baseline efficacy, safety, durability, correction, retraction, or registry-result delta beyond the exPDite-2 activity-date refresh.

known_gaps.description: The current human_functional_benefit stage rests on one directly aging-relevant frailty MSC trial plus disease-specific replacement or transplant programs, not on replicated broad aging, intrinsic-capacity, healthy-lifespan, or mortality benefit.
known_gaps.suggested_action: Keep confidence low and require independent older-adult functional replication or disease-specific controlled outcomes with durable benefit before strengthening the public stage.

known_gaps.description: The strongest aging-relevant laromestrocel signal may reflect paracrine, immunovascular, or tissue-support effects rather than durable engraftment or restoration of aged stem-cell reserves.
known_gaps.suggested_action: Treat MSC frailty evidence as functional but mechanistically ambiguous, and require graft, lineage, persistence, target-engagement, or reserve-restoration evidence before making stronger replacement claims.

known_gaps.description: Replacement and transplant strategies carry surgery, conditioning, immunosuppression, rejection, infection, dyskinesia, ectopic differentiation, tumor, graft-behavior, and long-term durability concerns that are not deeply resolved in the current source map.
known_gaps.suggested_action: Do not assign LEV-relevant timing or stronger confidence without longer-term safety, durability, and risk-benefit evidence in the intended disease or aging population.

known_gaps.description: Parkinson, retinal disease, and multiple-sclerosis transplant evidence shows bounded disease-specific benefit or signals, but it does not establish general reversal of stem-cell exhaustion or scalable aging intervention utility.
known_gaps.suggested_action: Keep disease-specific replacement records as support for bounded human feasibility and function, not as broad geroscience efficacy.

known_gaps.description: The key exPDite-2 bemdaneprocel phase 3 registry remains recruiting with no posted results, and laromestrocel frailty replication or longer-term follow-up is not yet represented.
known_gaps.suggested_action: Continue ordinary surveillance for exPDite-2 status or results, laromestrocel replication, and posted results from adjacent Parkinson, retinal, frailty, or HSCT registries.

known_gaps.description: The assessed artifacts do not map animal lifespan, healthspan, stem-cell reserve, niche, immune-reconstitution, neural graft, or tissue-replacement models that could help bound LEV relevance.
known_gaps.suggested_action: If source-completeness work is scheduled, decide whether animal replacement and stem-cell-exhaustion models should be added as mechanistic or boundary support.

known_gaps.description: The June 6 surveillance pass found review-level context, but no formal triangulation across recent MSC frailty, Parkinson graft, retinal graft, HSCT, regenerative medicine, or stem-cell-exhaustion reviews is recorded.
known_gaps.suggested_action: Use a future source-completeness pass to compare recent reviews, guidelines, and meta-analyses against the current six-source public map.

known_gaps.description: The track overlaps heavily with stem-cell rejuvenation, EV or secretome therapy, regenerative medicine, organoid or tissue engineering, immune reset, and disease-specific transplantation, creating risk of overbroad aging interpretation.
known_gaps.suggested_action: Keep assigning records by mechanism, product, route, endpoint, and aging relevance, and preserve exPDite-2 as activity-only until result-bearing evidence appears.

next_coverage_action: Continue ordinary surveillance for exPDite-2 results or status changes, laromestrocel frailty replication, longer-term graft safety or durability, controlled Parkinson and retinal replacement outcomes, HSCT risk-benefit updates, and stem-cell-exhaustion aging relevance. Reserve coverage repair for a later source-completeness pass focused on animal healthspan models, formal review triangulation, registry network checks, observational follow-up, and seminal-anchor auditing if no material delta appears.
